questions.question_text: Which one of the following soft-tissue injuries involves a direct blunt impact that causes bleeding under intact skin without any open wound?
questions.explanation: A contusion is a blunt-force injury that ruptures small blood vessels beneath intact skin, producing bruising and swelling; abrasions, lacerations and punctures all involve breaks in the skin surface.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Abrasion involves superficial skin scraping.
Laceration is a torn, jagged open wound.
Puncture is a deep narrow penetration of the skin.

questions.question_text: An abrasion in sport is best defined as which of the following superficial types of soft-tissue injury on the body?

options.text: Superficial scraping of skin against a rough surface
Sharp penetration by a pointed object

questions.explanation: An abrasion is a graze caused when the outer layers of skin are rubbed or scraped against a rough surface, typically during a fall; it is superficial and usually heals without scarring.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: A deep cut reaching muscle is an incised wound, not abrasion.
Penetration by a pointed object is a puncture wound.
Ligament overstretching describes a sprain, not a skin injury.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements most accurately distinguishes a sprain from a strain in the context of sports injury classification?

options.text: A sprain injures a ligament; a strain injures a muscle or tendon
A sprain injures bone; a strain injures cartilage
A sprain injures nerves; a strain injures blood vessels
A sprain injures skin; a strain injures a joint capsule

questions.explanation: A sprain is the overstretching or tearing of a ligament at a joint, classically the ankle; a strain is a similar injury but to a muscle fibre or its tendon, such as hamstring or calf strains in sprinters.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Bone and cartilage injuries have their own distinct names.
Nerve and blood vessel injuries are not called sprain/strain.
Skin damage is classified as a wound, not sprain or strain.

questions.question_text: A football player suffers a fracture where the broken bone pierces through the overlying skin to become visible; this fracture is best classified as which of the following?
questions.explanation: A compound (open) fracture is defined by a break in the bone accompanied by a wound where the bone end pierces or is exposed through the skin; this carries high infection risk and requires urgent surgical management.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Simple fractures keep the skin intact with no wound.
Greenstick fractures are incomplete, typically in children.
Stress fractures are tiny cracks from repetitive overuse.

questions.question_text: A greenstick fracture is most commonly seen in which specific category of athletes, due to the particular nature of their developing skeletal system?

options.text: Young children with soft pliable bones

questions.explanation: Greenstick fractures occur when a soft, pliable bone bends and cracks on one side without breaking fully through, resembling a snapped green twig; they are characteristic of young children whose bones are not yet fully mineralised.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Elderly bones are brittle and break fully rather than bend.
Post-pubertal skeletons are too mineralised for greenstick.
Middle-aged bones behave like adult bone tissue.

questions.question_text: A marathon runner presents with gradually worsening shin pain that increases with training mileage and is tender on one small bony spot; which injury is most likely?

options.text: Acute muscle contusion of the calf

questions.explanation: A stress fracture is a hairline crack caused by repetitive submaximal loading that exceeds the bone's remodelling capacity; it presents with gradual, pinpoint pain that worsens with activity, typical of runners' tibia.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Compound fractures present acutely with visible bone.
Muscle contusions cause diffuse, not pinpoint, tenderness.
The tibia itself is a bone; ligaments are separate structures.

questions.question_text: Dislocation in a sports context is best defined as which of the following structural disturbances occurring at a joint during activity?

options.text: Complete displacement of articulating bone ends out of joint
Inflammation of the joint capsule without displacement
Rupture of a tendon at its insertion point
Partial tear within a single muscle fibre

questions.explanation: A dislocation is the complete displacement of the articulating ends of bones at a joint so that the normal contact between articular surfaces is lost, often accompanied by ligament and capsule damage and visible deformity.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Capsule inflammation alone is called capsulitis, not dislocation.
Tendon rupture is tendinous, not an articular displacement.
Muscle fibre tear is a strain, not a joint dislocation.

questions.question_text: Which joint is most commonly dislocated during contact sports such as kabaddi, wrestling and rugby due to its wide range of motion and shallow socket?
questions.explanation: The shoulder (glenohumeral) joint has the widest range of motion in the body but a shallow glenoid socket, making it the most frequently dislocated joint in contact sports, typically anteriorly, after forced abduction-external-rotation trauma.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Hip sockets are deep and highly stable; dislocation is rare.
Ankle injuries are usually sprains, not dislocations.
Knee dislocations are severe but uncommon in contact sport.

questions.question_text: Which of the following acute sports-injury scenarios is most consistent with a Grade II (moderate) ligament sprain of the ankle sustained during landing?

options.text: Minor stretch with no laxity and full function
Partial ligament tear with moderate laxity and painful weight bearing
Complete ligament rupture with gross instability
Bony avulsion with visible deformity only

questions.explanation: Ligament sprains are graded I (mild stretch, no laxity), II (partial tear with some laxity, moderate pain and swelling, painful weight bearing) and III (complete rupture with gross instability); the scenario matches Grade II.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: No laxity with full function describes Grade I sprain.
Gross instability indicates a complete Grade III rupture.
Bony avulsion is a fracture variant, not a pure sprain.

questions.question_text: Which of the following combinations of intrinsic factors in an athlete most strongly predisposes to repeated soft-tissue injuries during high-intensity training cycles?

options.text: Well-designed equipment and smooth playing surface
Poor flexibility, muscle imbalance and inadequate warm-up
High aerobic fitness and balanced nutrition
Correct technique and adequate sleep routines

questions.explanation: Reduced joint flexibility, muscular imbalance around joints and insufficient warm-up before high-intensity loading place soft tissues under non-uniform stress, increasing the risk of strains, sprains and contusions in training.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Good equipment and surfaces reduce, not raise, injury risk.
High fitness and nutrition are protective against injury.
Correct technique and sleep help recovery and injury prevention.

questions.question_text: Match each soft-tissue injury in Column A with its most accurate defining feature listed in Column B and choose the correct mapping option.

column_b.text: Overstretching or tearing of a ligament
Bleeding under intact skin from blunt force
Overstretch or tear of muscle / tendon
Superficial scraping of the skin surface

questions.explanation: Contusion is subcutaneous bleeding from blunt force; abrasion is superficial skin scraping; strain is muscle or tendon overstretch/tear; sprain is ligament overstretch/tear — four distinct soft-tissue injury types.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Contusion is always a closed, bruise-type injury.
Strain involves muscle/tendon, never ligament.
Sprain is specifically a ligament overstretch/tear.

questions.question_text: Match each fracture type in Column A with its most accurate defining description listed in Column B and select the correct mapping below.

column_b.text: Incomplete break, bone bends on one side
Clean break with skin intact over the bone
Bone pierces through the overlying skin

questions.explanation: Simple fractures leave the skin intact; compound fractures pierce the skin; greenstick fractures are incomplete bends typical of children; stress fractures are hairline cracks from repetitive overloading.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Compound fractures must involve exposed bone.
Greenstick fractures are incomplete, not clean breaks.
Stress fractures come from repetition, not single trauma.

questions.question_text: Match each injury in Column A with the sport in which it is most characteristically encountered from Column B, and choose the correct combination from the options given.
questions.explanation: Tibial stress fractures typify endurance runners; shoulder dislocations are common in grappling sports like wrestling and kabaddi; hamstring strains peak in sprinters; lateral ankle sprains are classic landing injuries in basketball and volleyball.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Stress fractures are overuse injuries in endurance sports.
Shoulder dislocations dominate grappling contact sports.
Lateral ankle sprains occur on landing from jumps.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about ankle sprains carefully and select the option that best describes their logical relationship in the sports-injury context.
questions.assertion: Lateral ankle sprains are the most common acute injuries in basketball, volleyball and kabaddi players.
questions.reason: Landing on an inverted and plantarflexed foot stretches the lateral ligament complex beyond its elastic limit.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Inversion with plantarflexion loads the anterior talofibular and calcaneofibular ligaments beyond their elastic limit; this mechanism, common on jump landings and sudden cuts, explains the high lateral ankle sprain incidence in jumping sports.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains the injury mechanism.
Both statements are well-supported by sports-medicine evidence.
The assertion about jumping-sport ankle sprains is accurate.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason regarding stress fractures carefully and choose the option that correctly describes their logical connection.
questions.assertion: Stress fractures usually present with a gradual onset of localised pain rather than a sudden sharp injury event.
questions.reason: They arise from repetitive submaximal loading that outpaces the bone's remodelling capacity over time.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Because stress fractures evolve as microdamage from repeated loading accumulates faster than osteoblastic remodelling can repair, symptoms emerge gradually rather than abruptly; this mechanism explains their insidious presentation.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Gradual onset is a hallmark of overuse injuries.
Bone remodelling physiology explains the accumulation.
Both statements are textbook accurate.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason about dislocations in sports and select the option that correctly represents their logical relationship in clinical practice.
questions.assertion: Only a trained medical professional should attempt to reduce a suspected joint dislocation on the field.
questions.reason: Dislocations always heal completely within minutes without any risk of further tissue damage.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: The assertion is correct because improper reduction can worsen neurovascular injury and fracture; however the reason is false — dislocations damage ligaments, capsule and sometimes nerves or vessels and do not resolve spontaneously without care.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Trained reduction is indeed the safe standard of care.
Dislocations carry real risk of nerve and vessel injury.
Spontaneous complete healing in minutes is clinically false.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following physiological events of the acute inflammatory response to a sports-related soft-tissue injury in their correct chronological order.

items.text: Tissue trauma and release of chemical mediators
Migration of phagocytic cells to the injured area
Swelling, redness, heat and pain at the site

questions.explanation: Tissue trauma first releases chemical mediators like histamine and bradykinin; these cause vasodilation and capillary permeability; fluid leakage produces the cardinal signs of inflammation; phagocytes then migrate to clear debris.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Tissue trauma must precede mediator-driven vasodilation.
Cardinal signs appear after vascular changes occur.
Phagocyte migration follows, not precedes, inflammation.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following overlapping phases of soft-tissue healing following a muscle strain into the correct chronological order observed biologically.

items.text: Haemostasis with platelet plug and clot
Inflammatory phase with immune cell activity
Remodelling and maturation of scar tissue

questions.explanation: Soft-tissue healing begins with haemostasis forming a platelet plug, followed by the inflammatory phase with neutrophils and macrophages, then proliferation laying down collagen and new vessels, and finally long remodelling of scar tissue.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Haemostasis is the first event after tissue damage.
Inflammation precedes fibroblast proliferation.
Remodelling is the slowest, final phase of healing.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: A sprain involves overstretching or tearing of a muscle, whereas a strain involves a ligament injury at a joint.
questions.explanation: The statement reverses the definitions; in fact, a sprain is a ligament injury at a joint while a strain involves a muscle or tendon; remembering 'ligament-sprain, muscle-strain' prevents confusion in exams.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Sprain specifically denotes ligament injury.
Strain specifically denotes muscle or tendon injury.
The statement has the two terms reversed.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram classifying common sports injuries by tissue type, and identify the tissue category to which a sprain of the anterior talofibular ligament belongs.
questions.explanation: A sprain by definition is overstretching or tearing of a ligament; the anterior talofibular ligament is a lateral ankle ligament, so its sprain falls under the soft-tissue → ligament injury sub-category rather than bone, joint or skin.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The structure injured is ligament, not bone.
No loss of joint articulation means no dislocation.
The injury is internal, not a skin abrasion.
